disease.name: Obstructive Sleep Apnoea
disease.note: OSA is characterised by repetitive partial or complete upper airway collapse during sleep, causing intermittent hypoxia and sleep fragmentation. STOP-BANG and Epworth Sleepiness Scale screen at-risk populations. Polysomnography (AHI) confirms diagnosis. CPAP/BiPAP is first-line therapy.

qualitativeModifiers.description: Loud snoring reported by bed partner

historyFeatures.note: Bed partner reports pauses in breathing during sleep
Patient wakes with sensation of choking or gasping
Increased atrial natriuretic peptide from hypoxia
Associated with increased negative intrathoracic pressure

examFeatures.note: BMI >30 is strong risk factor; neck circumference >40 cm (M) or >36 cm (F) increases risk
Craniofacial abnormalities reduce posterior airway space
Mallampati grade 3–4 predicts OSA in children and adults

riskFactors.note: BMI >30 increases risk 2–5 fold; central obesity most predictive
Men 2–3 times more likely than premenopausal women
Especially in children; adenotonsillectomy is curative in many

subtypes.name: Adult Obstructive Sleep Apnoea
subtypes.management: CPAP first-line for moderate–severe OSA (AHI ≥15 or AHI 5–14 with symptoms/comorbidities); BiPAP if CPAP fails or OHS overlap; weight loss; MAD (mandibular advancement device) for mild–moderate or CPAP intolerant; hypoglossal nerve stimulation if CPAP fails

subtypes.name: Paediatric Obstructive Sleep Apnoea
subtypes.management: Adenotonsillectomy first-line; CPAP if residual OSA post-surgery; weight management; anti-inflammatory therapy (nasal steroids, montelukast)

subtypes.name: OSA-OHS Overlap Syndrome (Obesity Hypoventilation Syndrome)
subtypes.management: BiPAP (not CPAP) to manage hypercapnia; weight loss; aggressive oxygen therapy; treatment of cor pulmonale; consider GLP-1 agonists for weight loss

subtypes.name: Positional OSA
subtypes.management: Positional therapy (sleeping on side, tennis ball technique, vibrating devices); avoid supine sleep

bedside.name: Epworth Sleepiness Scale (ESS)
bedside.indication: assess daytime sleepiness; score ≥11 suggests pathological sleepiness

bedside.name: STOP-BANG questionnaire
bedside.indication: validated OSA screening; ≥3 points high risk; ≥5 moderate-severe OSA likelihood
bedside.note: Snoring, Tiredness, Observed apnea, Pressure (HTN), BMI >35, Age >50, Neck circumference >40 cm, Gender male

bedside.name: Pulse oximetry nocturnal
bedside.indication: desaturation index may suggest OSA severity

laboratory.name: Thyroid function (TSH)
laboratory.indication: hypothyroidism can cause or worsen OSA

imaging.name: Polysomnography (PSG) Level 1
imaging.indication: definitive diagnosis; measures AHI (apnoea-hypopnoea index), oxygen nadir, sleep architecture, limb movements, ECG
imaging.note: AHI 5–14: mild; 15–30: moderate; >30: severe. Also scored as arousals per hour

imaging.name: Home sleep apnoea test (HSAT) Level 3
imaging.priority: if high pre-test probability and no comorbidities
imaging.indication: limited channels (flow, effort, oximetry); acceptable alternative per AASM guidelines

imaging.name: Upper airway assessment (nasendoscopy, DISE)
imaging.indication: drug-induced sleep endoscopy (DISE) visualises collapse site for targeted surgery or hypoglossal nerve stimulation

advanced.name: Multiple Sleep Latency Test (MSLT)
advanced.note: Mean sleep latency <8 min pathological; SOREM ≥2 suggests narcolepsy

advanced.name: Echocardiogram
advanced.indication: if pulmonary hypertension or right heart strain suspected

management.protocolReference: AASM Clinical Practice Guidelines for OSA (2023); BTS/NICE Guidelines for Sleep Apnoea

pathways.criteria: AHI 5–14, Epworth <11, no cardiovascular comorbidity, minimal hypoxia
treatment: Lifestyle modification: weight loss (≥10% reduction significantly improves AHI and symptoms per AASM)
Exercise programme (even without weight loss improves AHI)
Alcohol avoidance 3–4 hours before bedtime
Sleep positioning: avoid supine sleep (positional therapy if AHI normalises in non-supine)
Sleep hygiene optimisation (adequate sleep duration, consistent schedule)
Mandibular advancement device (MAD) if patient prefers device therapy
Re-evaluate response in 3–6 months
pathways.followUp: Repeat ESS and consider follow-up PSG if symptoms persist

pathways.criteria: AHI 15–30 with symptoms (ESS ≥11) or cardiovascular comorbidity
treatment: CPAP (continuous positive airway pressure) first-line therapy per AASM
Auto-CPAP or fixed CPAP: titrated pressure 5–20 cmH2O to abolish apnoeas/hypopnoeas
CPAP adherence ≥4 hours/night for ≥70% of nights improves outcomes
Heated humidification and mask fitting optimisation to improve tolerance
Weight loss programme (refer to bariatric medicine if BMI >35)
Consider MAD as alternative if CPAP intolerant or declined
Nasal corticosteroids if concomitant allergic rhinitis
pathways.followUp: CPAP download review at 1 month, then 3–6 monthly. Repeat PSG if issues. ESS reassessment.

pathways.severity: severe (AHI >30 or AHI >15 with severe hypoxia/complications)
pathways.criteria: AHI >30, or AHI >15 with severe desaturation (<80%), OHS, cor pulmonale, or refractory hypertension
treatment: CPAP or BiPAP (if OHS overlap: BiPAP-S/T to address hypercapnia)
CPAP titration PSG to determine optimal pressure (manually supervised titration preferred)
If CPAP fails: evaluate for hypoglossal nerve stimulation (Inspire device) - suitable if non-obese with specific collapse pattern (concentric complete collapse excluded)
Surgical options: uvulopalatopharyngoplasty (UPPP) - variable success; maxillomandibular advancement (MMA) - high success but invasive
Tonsillectomy if tonsillar hypertrophy is the primary cause
Bariatric surgery evaluation if BMI ≥40 or ≥35 with comorbidities
Oxygen supplementation if significant nocturnal hypoxemia despite optimal PAP therapy
Treat complications: hypertension (ACE-i/ARB often first-line), arrhythmias, right heart failure
pathways.escalation: BiPAP for persistent hypercapnia; hypoglossal nerve stimulation or maxillomandibular advancement for CPAP failure in selected patients

treatment: Adenotonsillectomy first-line (cure rate 80–90% in otherwise healthy children)
If residual OSA post-surgery or contraindication to surgery: CPAP
Nasal corticosteroids (fluticasone) + montelukast for mild OSA if adenotonsillar hypertrophy not severe
Weight management if obesity present
Monitor for growth, school performance, behavioural issues
pathways.followUp: Post-operative PSG in 3–6 months if severe OSA, obesity, or symptoms persist

complications.note: OSA is the most common cause of resistant hypertension
AF recurrence after cardioversion is higher with untreated OSA
Untreated OSA increases accident risk 2–7 fold

disease.emergencyPriority: if severe OSA with cor pulmonale, daytime hypercapnia, or severe bradyarrhythmia
diagnosticClues: loud habitual snoring with witnessed apnoeas
AHI ≥5 on PSG with compatible symptoms or comorbidities
nocturnal oxygen desaturation with cyclic pattern
exclusionClues: negative home sleep apnoea test in high-risk patient requires in-laboratory PSG for definitive exclusion
pure insomnia without snoring or witnessed apnoeas
PSG with AHI <5 effectively excludes OSA in symptomatic patients
cataplexy and sleep-onset REM on MSLT suggest narcolepsy rather than OSA
